title: Rates of Change in Science and Engineering
objectives: Define velocity and acceleration as derivatives of position, and calculate them for objects in motion
Apply exponential growth models to analyze population dynamics in biology
Calculate and interpret marginal cost, revenue, and profit in economic contexts
Use derivatives to analyze reaction rates in chemistry and current flow in electrical circuits

devotional.title: The Language of Creation
devotional.content: "He makes grass grow for the cattle, and plants for people to cultivate—bringing forth food from the earth." The derivative appears everywhere in God's creation—in the motion of the planets, the growth of populations, the flow of rivers, and the pulse of electrical current. This universality reveals a deep unity in nature, as though the Creator wove a single thread of mathematical logic through all of creation. As we learn to read this mathematical language, we gain insight into the wisdom embedded in the natural world.

content.text: *"The book of nature is written in the language of mathematics."* —Galileo Galilei

You have spent six sections learning *how* to differentiate. You can handle polynomials, exponentials, trigonometric functions, logarithms, compositions, and implicit curves. Now we ask the deeper question: **what does the derivative *mean*?**
*"There is a time for everything, and a season for every activity under the heavens."* —Ecclesiastes 3:1

The answer, of course, is *rate of change*. That was the definition all along. But the phrase "rate of change" takes on strikingly different meanings depending on whether you are a physicist tracking a falling stone, a biologist culturing bacteria, an economist analyzing production costs, or an engineer designing a circuit. In every case, the mathematics is the same—the derivative—but the interpretation changes with the context.

This section is a guided tour through several scientific disciplines. Our goal is twofold: to see the derivative at work in the real world, and to practice the differentiation rules of this chapter on functions that arise naturally in science and engineering.
Physics: Velocity and Acceleration
Suppose an object moves along a straight line (say, a ball tossed straight up or a car traveling down a highway). Let $s(t)$ denote the **position** of the object at time $t$.

content.title: Velocity and Acceleration
content.content:
If $s(t)$ gives the position of an object at time $t$, then:

(i) The **velocity** is $v(t) = s'(t) = \frac{ds}{dt}$.

(ii) The **acceleration** is $a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t) = \frac{d^2s}{dt^2}$.

(iii) The **speed** is $|v(t)|$.

content.text: Velocity tells us the *rate* at which position changes, and its sign tells us the *direction*: positive velocity means the object is moving in the positive direction; negative velocity means it is moving in the negative direction. Acceleration tells us the rate at which *velocity* changes.
*"Mathematics is the language in which God has written the universe."* —Galileo Galilei

content.title: Free-fall under gravity
content.problem:
A ball is dropped from the top of a 200-foot building. Ignoring air resistance, the height of the ball above the ground at time $t$ seconds is

$$s(t) = 200 - 16t^2.$$

Find: (a) the velocity after 2 seconds, (b) the acceleration, and (c) the time at which the ball hits the ground and its speed at that moment.
content.solution:
**(a)** We differentiate using the Power Rule and the Constant Rule:

$$v(t) = s'(t) = \frac{d}{dt}[200 - 16t^2] = -32t.$$

At $t = 2$: $v(2) = -32(2) = -64$ ft/s. The negative sign tells us the ball is falling (moving in the negative direction, i.e., downward).

**(b)** Differentiating again:

$$a(t) = v'(t) = \frac{d}{dt}[-32t] = -32 \text{ ft/s}^2.$$

The acceleration is constant—this is Earth's gravitational acceleration (approximately $-32$ ft/s², or $-9.8$ m/s² in metric units).

**(c)** The ball hits the ground when $s(t) = 0$:

$$200 - 16t^2 = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t^2 = \frac{200}{16} = 12.5 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t = \sqrt{12.5} = \frac{5\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 3.54 \text{ s}.$$

The velocity at impact is

$$v\!\left(\frac{5\sqrt{2}}{2}\right) = -32 \cdot \frac{5\sqrt{2}}{2} = -80\sqrt{2} \approx -113.1 \text{ ft/s}.$$

The *speed* at impact is $|v| = 80\sqrt{2} \approx 113.1$ ft/s.

content.title: Projectile motion
content.problem:
A model rocket is launched vertically with an initial velocity of $128$ ft/s from ground level. Its height at time $t$ is given by

$$s(t) = 128t - 16t^2.$$

Find (a) the maximum height and (b) the total time of flight.
content.solution:
**(a)** The rocket reaches its maximum height when $v(t) = 0$:

$$v(t) = s'(t) = 128 - 32t = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t = 4 \text{ s}.$$

The maximum height is $s(4) = 128(4) - 16(16) = 512 - 256 = 256$ ft.

We can verify this is a maximum (not a minimum) by checking that $a(t) = s''(t) = -32 < 0$: the acceleration is negative, so the velocity is decreasing. At $t = 4$ the velocity passes through zero from positive to negative, confirming a peak.

**(b)** The rocket returns to the ground when $s(t) = 0$:

$$128t - 16t^2 = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 16t(8 - t) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t = 0 \text{ or } t = 8.$$

The total time of flight is $8$ seconds. Notice the symmetry: the rocket takes $4$ seconds to go up and $4$ seconds to come back down.

content.text: Biology: Population Growth
*"God blessed them and said, 'Be fruitful and increase in number and fill the water in the seas, and let the birds increase on the earth.'"* —Genesis 1:22

In biology, one of the most fundamental quantities is a **population** $P(t)$ as a function of time. The derivative $\frac{dP}{dt}$ measures the **growth rate**—how fast the population is increasing (or decreasing) at a given instant.

The simplest model of population growth is **exponential growth**, in which the rate of growth is proportional to the current population:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = kP,$$

where $k > 0$ is a constant called the **growth rate constant**. The solution to this equation (which we will derive rigorously in a later chapter) is

$$P(t) = P_0 e^{kt},$$

where $P_0 = P(0)$ is the initial population.

Let us verify this: if $P(t) = P_0 e^{kt}$, then by the Chain Rule,

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = P_0 \cdot k e^{kt} = k \cdot P_0 e^{kt} = kP(t). \quad\checkmark$$

content.title: Bacterial growth
content.problem: A bacterial colony starts with $500$ cells and doubles every $3$ hours. Find (a) the growth rate constant $k$, (b) the population after $10$ hours, and (c) the rate of growth after $10$ hours.
content.solution:
**(a)** We have $P_0 = 500$ and $P(3) = 1000$. Setting up:

$$1000 = 500 e^{3k} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 2 = e^{3k} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 3k = \ln 2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k = \frac{\ln 2}{3} \approx 0.2310.$$

**(b)** After $10$ hours:

$$P(10) = 500 e^{10 \cdot (\ln 2)/3} = 500 e^{(10\ln 2)/3} = 500 \cdot 2^{10/3} \approx 500 \cdot 10.079 \approx 5040 \text{ cells}.$$

**(c)** The growth rate is

$$\left.\frac{dP}{dt}\right|_{t=10} = kP(10) = \frac{\ln 2}{3} \cdot 5040 \approx 0.2310 \cdot 5040 \approx 1164 \text{ cells/hour}.$$

The colony is adding roughly $1164$ new cells per hour at the $10$-hour mark.

content.title: Exponential growth cannot last forever
content.content: The model $P(t) = P_0e^{kt}$ predicts unbounded growth. In reality, populations encounter limited resources, disease, and competition. More realistic models (like the **logistic model**, which you will meet in differential equations) account for a carrying capacity. The exponential model is excellent for *early-stage* growth when resources are plentiful.

content.text: Economics: Marginal Analysis

Economists use derivatives constantly, though they call them by different names. If $C(x)$ is the total cost of producing $x$ units of a good, then the derivative $C'(x)$ is called the **marginal cost**.

content.title: Marginal Cost, Revenue, and Profit
content.content:
Let $x$ denote the number of units produced (or sold).

(i) If $C(x)$ is the **total cost** function, then $C'(x)$ is the **marginal cost**—the approximate cost of producing one additional unit.

(ii) If $R(x)$ is the **total revenue** function, then $R'(x)$ is the **marginal revenue**—the approximate additional revenue from selling one more unit.

(iii) The **profit** function is $P(x) = R(x) - C(x)$, and the **marginal profit** is $P'(x) = R'(x) - C'(x)$.

content.text: *"Economics is the study of mankind in the ordinary business of life."* —Alfred Marshall

Why does $C'(x)$ approximate the cost of one more unit? Because

$$C'(x) \approx \frac{C(x+1) - C(x)}{1} = C(x+1) - C(x).$$

The derivative is the limit of this difference quotient as the increment approaches zero, so for integer values of $x$, $C'(x)$ is a close approximation to the actual cost of the $(x+1)$-st unit.

content.title: Marginal cost and revenue
content.problem:
A small company manufactures artisanal candles. The cost (in dollars) of producing $x$ candles per week is

$$C(x) = 0.001x^3 - 0.15x^2 + 12x + 800,$$

and the revenue from selling $x$ candles at price $p(x) = 25 - 0.02x$ dollars each is

$$R(x) = x \cdot p(x) = 25x - 0.02x^2.$$

Find (a) the marginal cost and marginal revenue when $x = 100$, and (b) the production level at which profit is maximized (approximately).
content.solution:
**(a)** Differentiating:

$$C'(x) = 0.003x^2 - 0.30x + 12,$$
$$R'(x) = 25 - 0.04x.$$

At $x = 100$:

$$C'(100) = 0.003(10000) - 0.30(100) + 12 = 30 - 30 + 12 = \$12,$$
$$R'(100) = 25 - 0.04(100) = 25 - 4 = \$21.$$

The $101$st candle costs approximately $\$12$ to produce but brings in approximately $\$21$ in revenue. Since $R'(100) > C'(100)$, the company should increase production.

**(b)** Profit is maximized (approximately) where marginal revenue equals marginal cost:

$$R'(x) = C'(x) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 25 - 0.04x = 0.003x^2 - 0.30x + 12.$$

Rearranging:

$$0.003x^2 - 0.26x - 13 = 0.$$

By the quadratic formula:

$$x = \frac{0.26 \pm \sqrt{0.0676 + 0.156}}{0.006} = \frac{0.26 \pm \sqrt{0.2236}}{0.006} = \frac{0.26 \pm 0.4729}{0.006}.$$

Taking the positive root: $x \approx \frac{0.7329}{0.006} \approx 122$ candles.

content.text: Chemistry: Reaction Rates
*"When he established the force of the wind and measured out the waters, when he made a decree for the rain and a path for the thunderstorm—then he looked at wisdom and appraised it."* —Job 28:25–27

In chemistry, a **reaction rate** measures how fast the concentration of a reactant decreases (or a product increases) over time. If $[A](t)$ denotes the concentration of substance $A$ at time $t$ (usually in mol/L), then the rate of reaction is

$$\text{rate} = -\frac{d[A]}{dt}.$$

The negative sign is a convention: since the reactant is being consumed, $\frac{d[A]}{dt} < 0$, and we want the rate to be positive.

content.title: First-order reaction
content.problem:
In a first-order decomposition, the concentration of a reactant decays exponentially:

$$[A](t) = [A]_0\, e^{-kt},$$

where $[A]_0$ is the initial concentration and $k > 0$ is the rate constant. Suppose the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) in a solution has $[A]_0 = 0.80$ mol/L and $k = 0.035$ min⁻¹. Find the reaction rate at $t = 20$ minutes.
content.solution:
Using the Chain Rule:

$$\frac{d[A]}{dt} = [A]_0 \cdot (-k) e^{-kt} = -k \cdot [A]_0\, e^{-kt}.$$

The reaction rate is therefore

$$\text{rate} = -\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k \cdot [A]_0\, e^{-kt}.$$

At $t = 20$:

$$\text{rate} = 0.035 \cdot 0.80 \cdot e^{-0.035(20)} = 0.028 \cdot e^{-0.70} \approx 0.028 \cdot 0.4966 \approx 0.0139 \text{ mol/(L·min)}.$$

The concentration is decreasing at about $0.014$ mol/L per minute at the $20$-minute mark.

content.text: Electrical Engineering: Current and Charge

In an electrical circuit, **current** is the rate at which charge flows past a point. If $Q(t)$ denotes the charge (in coulombs) that has flowed past a point at time $t$ (in seconds), then the current is

$$I(t) = \frac{dQ}{dt}.$$

Current is measured in **amperes** (A), where $1$ A $= 1$ C/s.

content.title: Current from a charge function
content.problem:
The charge flowing through a component is given by

$$Q(t) = 5\sin(120\pi t) + 3t \quad\text{(coulombs)},$$

where $t$ is in seconds. Find the current $I(t)$ and evaluate $I(1/240)$.
content.solution:
Differentiating using the Chain Rule and the Power Rule:

$$I(t) = \frac{dQ}{dt} = 5 \cdot \cos(120\pi t) \cdot 120\pi + 3 = 600\pi\cos(120\pi t) + 3.$$

At $t = 1/240$:

$$I\!\left(\frac{1}{240}\right) = 600\pi\cos\!\left(\frac{120\pi}{240}\right) + 3 = 600\pi\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) + 3 = 600\pi(0) + 3 = 3 \text{ A}.$$

At this instant the sinusoidal component of the current is zero, and only the steady $3$ A contribution remains.

content.text: *"Nothing is too wonderful to be true, if it be consistent with the laws of nature."* —Michael Faraday
A Unifying Perspective
Let us pause and notice something remarkable. In each discipline above, we performed the same mathematical operation—differentiation—but the interpretation changed entirely:
*"For since the creation of the world God's invisible qualities—his eternal power and divine nature—have been clearly seen, being understood from what has been made."* —Romans 1:20

This universality is one of the deepest features of calculus. The same mathematical structure governs falling apples, growing colonies, business decisions, chemical reactions, and electrical circuits.
It is as though the Creator wove a single thread of logic through all of nature.
Exercises

content.problem: A stone is dropped from a bridge $400$ feet above a river. Its height at time $t$ seconds is $s(t) = 400 - 16t^{2}$. Find the velocity and speed of the stone after $3$ seconds.

A particle moves along a line with position $s(t) = t^{3} - 6t^{2} + 9t$ (meters, seconds). (a) Find $v(t)$ and $a(t)$. (b) When is the particle at rest? (c) When is the particle moving in the positive direction?
A ball is thrown upward from the ground with initial velocity $64$ ft/s. Its height is $s(t) = 64t - 16t^{2}$. Find the maximum height and the time at which the ball returns to the ground.
The position of a car on a highway is $s(t) = 60t + 5\sin t$ (feet, seconds). Find the velocity and acceleration functions. What is the maximum velocity?

A projectile is launched vertically so that its height (in meters) at time $t$ seconds is $s(t) = -4.9t^{2} + 49t + 2$. Find the velocity when the projectile is at its highest point. Confirm that $a(t) = -9.8$ m/s$^{2}$. %--- Biology: Population Growth ---
A bacterial colony starts with $2000$ cells and grows exponentially: $P(t) = 2000 e^{0.4t}$ (cells, hours). Find the growth rate $P'(t)$ and evaluate it at $t = 5$.
The population of a fish species in a lake is modeled by $P(t) = \frac{1200}{1 + 5e^{-0.3t}}$ (a logistic model). Find $P'(t)$ using the Quotient Rule and evaluate $P'(5)$.
A yeast culture has population $P(t) = 100 \cdot 2^{t/4}$ (cells, hours). Rewrite $P(t)$ using $e^{kt}$ form, find $k$, and compute $P'(8)$. %--- Economics: Marginal Analysis ---
The cost of producing $x$ units of a product is $C(x) = 5000 + 10x - 0.01x^{2}$ (dollars). Find the marginal cost at $x = 200$ and interpret its meaning.
A company's revenue function is $R(x) = 50x - 0.5x^{2}$ (dollars). Find the marginal revenue when $x = 30$ and when $x = 60$. At which production level is marginal revenue zero?

The demand for a product is $p(x) = 80 - 0.1x$ dollars per unit. The revenue function is $R(x) = x\,p(x)$. The cost function is $C(x) = 200 + 15x + 0.05x^{2}$. Find the production level at which $R'(x) = C'(x)$.

The profit from selling $x$ items is $P(x) = -0.02x^{2} + 30x - 4000$. Find the marginal profit and determine the production level at which marginal profit equals zero. %--- Chemistry and Engineering ---
A chemical reaction has concentration $[A](t) = 2.0\,e^{-0.05t}$ mol/L. Find the reaction rate at $t = 10$ minutes.
The charge through a wire is $Q(t) = t^{3} - 2t^{2} + 5t$ coulombs at time $t$ seconds. Find the current $I(t)$ and evaluate it at $t = 3$.

The voltage across a capacitor is $V(t) = 12(1 - e^{-t/RC})$ volts, where $R = 1000\;\Omega$ and $C = 0.001$ F. Find $\frac{dV}{dt}$ and evaluate it at $t = 0$ and $t = 2$. %--- Challenging Problems ---

A particle moves along a line with position $s(t) = te^{-t}$ for $t \geq 0$. (a) Find the velocity and acceleration. (b) When is the velocity zero? (c) Show that the particle never returns to the origin for $t > 0$.

A drug concentration in the bloodstream is modeled by $C(t) = 5t\,e^{-0.4t}$ mg/L, where $t$ is time in hours after injection. Find the time at which the concentration is maximum and the rate at which it is changing at $t = 3$ hours.
The total cost for a company is $C(x) = 500 + 20x\ln x$ for $x \geq 1$. Find the marginal cost and show that it is increasing for all $x \geq 1$.
In a second-order reaction, the concentration satisfies $[A](t) = \frac{[A]_{0}}{1 + [A]_{0}\,kt}$. Show that the rate of decrease of $[A]$ is $k[A]^{2}$.
The position of an oscillating spring is $s(t) = 3e^{-0.5t}\cos(4t)$ cm. Find the velocity $v(t)$ using the Product Rule and Chain Rule, and find $v(0)$.
An object moves so that its velocity is $v(t) = \frac{100}{1+t^{2}}$ m/s. Find the acceleration and determine when the object is decelerating (i.e., $a(t) < 0$). %--- Proof and Theoretical ---
Show that if $s(t) = at^{2} + bt + c$ (constant acceleration motion), then the acceleration $a(t) = s''(t) = 2a$ is indeed constant. What are $v(0)$ and $s(0)$ in terms of $a$, $b$, $c$?

Suppose $P(t) = P_{0}e^{kt}$. Prove that $\frac{P'(t)}{P(t)} = k$ for all $t$. This ratio is called the **relative growth rate**. Explain why it is constant for exponential growth. %--- Creation Reveals ---

The distance from the Sun to the Earth varies roughly as $r(t) = 1.496 \times 10^{8}\bigl(1 - 0.0167\cos(2\pi t/365)\bigr)$ km, where $t$ is measured in days. Find $r'(t)$ and determine on which day(s) of the year the Earth is moving fastest toward or away from the Sun (i.e., when $|r'(t)|$ is maximized).

The logistic model $P(t) = \frac{7.79}{1 + 6.1 e^{-0.02t}}$ (in billions) approximates world population since 1950 (with $t = 0$ corresponding to 1950). Find $P'(t)$ and evaluate $P'(70)$ (i.e., the growth rate in 2020). Express your answer in millions of people per year.
